summary:
The Roman-Sassanid Wars (224–651 AD) were the defining geopolitical conflict of late antiquity — four centuries of near-continuous warfare between the [[event:81|Roman/Byzantine Empire]] and the [[event:2059|Sassanid Persian Empire]] over Mesopotamia, Armenia, and ultimately Syria and Egypt. When the more aggressive Sassanids replaced the Parthians in 224 AD, they immediately launched campaigns against Rome, with Shapur I achieving the most humiliating Roman defeat since Carrhae: the capture of Emperor Valerian at the [[event:2826|Battle of Ctesiphon]] in 260 AD — the only time in history that a Roman emperor was captured alive.

The wars oscillated for centuries in a cycle of conquest and reconquest: Mesopotamia changed hands repeatedly, both empires suffered catastrophic defeats and achieved brilliant victories, and enormous sums were spent in mutual attrition. The last and most devastating phase came in the early 7th century under Khosrow II, who conquered Syria, Palestine (capturing Jerusalem and the True Cross relic in 614), Egypt, and much of Anatolia — apparently on the verge of destroying the Byzantine Empire. The Byzantine Emperor Heraclius launched a desperate counterattack (622–628 AD) that penetrated deep into Persian territory and forced a complete Sassanid withdrawal. This final, mutually exhausting war left both empires so weakened that neither could resist the explosive Arab Muslim expansion that began in 632 AD — the Sassanid Empire was destroyed entirely by 651 AD, while Byzantium lost all its recovered Near Eastern provinces within a decade.
